A sequence \(\{a_n\}\) is a list of numbers \(a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots\). The sequence \(a_n\), where \(a_n = n^2\) begins with the terms 1, 4, 9, ....

A geometric progression is a sequence of the form \(a\), \(ar^2\), \(ar^3\), ....

A set is countable if:

  • it is finite
  • or infinite and there exists a one-to-one correspondence between the set of positive integers and the set.

A set that is not countable is called uncountable.

The following notation represents the sum of the numbers \(a_1\), \(a_2\), ..., \(a_n\):$$\sum^n_{k=1} a_k = a_1 + a_2 + \cdots +a_n$$Here are some properties:$$\begin{align} \sum^n_{j=m} ka_j &= k\sum^n_{j=m} a_j \\ \sum^n_{j=m} (a_j+b_j) &= \sum^n_{j=m} a_j+ \sum^n_{j=m} b_j \\ \sum^n_{i=m}\sum^q_{j=p} (a_ib_j) &= \left( \sum^n_{i=m} a_i \right) \left( \sum^q_{j=p} b_j \right) \\ \sum^n_{i=m}\sum^q_{j=p} (a_ib_j) &= \sum^q_{j=p}\sum^n_{i=m} (a_ib_j) \end{align}$$The product of the numbers \(a_1\), \(a_2\), ..., \(a_n\) is denoted by:$$\prod^n_{j=1} a_j = a_1 a_2 \cdots a_n$$If \(n \ge 1\), then \(n!\) (read as "\(n\) factorial") is the product of the integers 1, 2, .., \(n\).

Also, \(0! = 1\).
Proof that \(\sqrt{3}\) is irrational using the well-ordering property.
